coredata.dc:title: Effects of coffee bean types on the characteristics of carbon dots and their use for manufacturing cellulose nanofibers-based films for active packaging of meat
coredata.dc:creator: Sul, Yoonjung
Khan, Ajahar
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: CDs (carbon dots) were made through a hydrothermal process using three different types of coffee grounds (raw, roasted, and spent), and their physicochemical and optical properties were thoroughly studied. CNF (cellulose nanofibers)-based active packaging films were made and characterized by incorporating the CDs. The introduction of CDs improved the tensile strength of the CNF-based films by 26.1% and provided excellent UV-blocking ability by blocking 98.8% of UV-B and 87.4% of UV-A without sacrificing the film’s transparency. The CD-loaded CNF films showed strong antioxidant properties with ABTS radical and DPPH radical removal rates of 98.2% and 78.8%, respectively. Additionally, these films showed effective antimicrobial potential and significantly reduced the progress of E. coli and L. monocytogenes after 12 h of exposure. Packaging tests were performed at 4 °C for 21 days, which depicted that minced pork packaged with the CD-added CNF films preserves the quality of packaged meat in terms of appearance, pH, total viable colonies, and TBARS (thiobarbituric acid reactive substances). It is expected that manufacturing functional fillers (CDs) using waste resources generated in the food industry and developing active packaging films using them will not only extend the shelf life of packaged foods but also help in resource recycling and solving environmental problems.

head.ce:title: Effects of coffee bean types on the characteristics of carbon dots and their use for manufacturing cellulose nanofibers-based films for active packaging of meat

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CDs (carbon dots) were made through a hydrothermal process using three different types of coffee grounds (raw, roasted, and spent), and their physicochemical and optical properties were thoroughly studied. CNF (cellulose nanofibers)-based active packaging films were made and characterized by incorporating the CDs. The introduction of CDs improved the tensile strength of the CNF-based films by 26.1% and provided excellent UV-blocking ability by blocking 98.8% of UV-B and 87.4% of UV-A without sacrificing the film’s transparency. The CD-loaded CNF films showed strong antioxidant properties with ABTS radical and DPPH radical removal rates of 98.2% and 78.8%, respectively. Additionally, these films showed effective antimicrobial potential and significantly reduced the progress of E. coli and L. monocytogenes after 12 h of exposure. Packaging tests were performed at 4 °C for 21 days, which depicted that minced pork packaged with the CD-added CNF films preserves the quality of packaged meat in terms of appearance, pH, total viable colonies, and TBARS (thiobarbituric acid reactive substances). It is expected that manufacturing functional fillers (CDs) using waste resources generated in the food industry and developing active packaging films using them will not only extend the shelf life of packaged foods but also help in resource recycling and solving environmental problems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: After slaughter, meat is susceptible to microbial contamination, undergoes certain biological processes (oxidation) and has a high moisture content, making it impossible to store even when refrigerated and causing microbial spoilage, discoloration and odor, affecting quality (Kong et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2023). Additionally, seasonal processing, storage, and transportation can further accelerate quality deterioration, shortening the shelf life of meat goods and reducing their economic value (Gil & Rudy, 2023). Currently, food preservation mainly involves oxidizing agents, preservatives, and other chemical approaches, and physical means include temperature control, irradiation, gas control, and ultra-high pressure methods (Freche et al., 2022; Gol et al., 2013). However, chemical methods have dangerous problems such as chronic food poisoning, malformation, and safety, while physical methods have problems such as huge investment, high operational requirements, and complex equipment. Therefore, there is a need to design innovative, sustainable, simple and safe active packaging resources to spread the shelf life and sustain the value of meat products (Siddiqui et al., 2023).

Most plastics used in food packaging are petroleum-based resources that do not decompose, are not recyclable, are toxic, and cause a number of environmental problems. Therefore, emphasis has been placed on finding eco-friendly and renewable alternatives to non-biodegradable packaging materials by limiting the use of non-renewable resources (Ezati et al., 2023a). Accordingly, the preservation of meat products using biomass-derived materials appears to be an effective and safe method that has currently received ample consideration (Khan et al., 2024; Khan et al., 2023c; Fan et al., 2020; Wagh et al., 2023). In this context, packaging materials made from polysaccharides such as cellulose have emerged to replace existing non-biodegradable plastic materials. Cellulose is produced from sustainable resources, is successfully recycled from existing biomass waste, and is considered cost-effective (Ezati et al., 2023b). In particular, cellulose nanofibers (CNF), which are attracting attention in the field of sustainable active packaging applications, were found to be suitable for being biodegradable, non-toxic, low-cost, biocompatible, and admirable film-developing ability (Wagh et al., 2023). However, compared to existing packaging materials, CNF-based films have the shortcomings of low UV resistance, poor mechanical properties (such as tensile strength, elastic modulus, and elongation at break), and lack of practical characteristics such as antioxidant and antibacterial potentials (Riahi et al., 2022). Therefore, CNFs have been united with other active materials to advance the practicality of packaging films with desired functional properties.

On the other hand, food waste and its disposal is a huge problem worldwide (Khan et al., 2023b). Producing high-value products using food waste is greatly beneficial to the environment and food recyclability. In this context, coffee, one of the most popular beverages, is consumed annually worldwide, with coffee consumption reaching 1 billion kg (Smrke et al., 2022), and a large number of coffee grounds are discarded after consumption. Research is currently underway to recycle used coffee grounds to produce value-added products such as low-grade fillers, antioxidants, and biodiesel production (Jin Ong et al., 2023). Additionally, coffee grounds contain a large amount of polyphenols, minerals, carbohydrates, and lipids, so they can be used to develop high-value-added products (Freitas et al., 2023). Coffee grounds can be a valuable cause for the construction of green CDs (carbon dots) owing to their low toxicity, better biocompatibility, and abundant carbon source (Khan et al., 2023a). CDs (<10 nm) are frequently employed as active food ingredients in films and food coatings due to their biocompatibility, good chemical stability, environmental sustainability, surface functionalization, low toxicity, ease of synthesis, good compatibility, and antibacterial and antioxidant properties. The excellent electron acceptor and electron donor functions of CDs account for their antioxidant properties (Tammina et al., 2023a). Additionally, CDs have abundant π-π conjugated groups at their core, providing excellent UV absorption performance (Khan et al., 2023a). CDs also have strong antibacterial ability, which is attributed to ROS generation and photothermal and photoelectric effects (Khan et al., 2023d).

Recently, Wagh et al. (2023) prepared CNF-based multifunctional films containing Brassica oleracea CDs and anthocyanins for active and intelligent packaging of fish, shrimp, and pork (Wagh et al., 2023). The CD and anthocyanin added-CNF film showed functions such as real-time freshness monitoring, enhanced storage, and shelf life extension of packaged meat. Another study showed that the minced meat’s shelf life was stretched by 3 days more using bacterial cellulose/CD-based activated food packaging membranes (Ghorbani et al., 2024). In addition, CDs can be useful not only for food preservation but also for food safety assessment (Fan et al., 2020).

This study introduces a groundbreaking solution that utilizes food industry waste (coffee grounds) to not only solve waste management problems but also advance the development of sustainable active packaging materials. We have utilized the untapped resource of food waste to creatively transform various forms of beverage waste into eco-friendly multi-functional fluorescent CDs. Herein, CDs were made using three different types of coffee grounds, i.e., raw, roasted, and spent coffee grounds, and their fluorescence properties, UV barrier ability, antioxidant efficacy towards DPPH and ABTS assay and antimicrobial action toward four bacterial strains were investigated. In addition, a CNF-based active packaging film containing three types of CDs was developed, its physicochemical characteristics were comprehensively assessed, and the effectiveness of the active packaging film was evaluated through a minced pork packaging test. This approach not only presents a solution for recycling food waste but also expands the scope of active packaging research and the development of new hybrid materials with desired functional properties.

TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofibers (CNF, length: 5–50 µm; width: 10–20 nm) were supplied by Moorim P&P Co., Ltd. (Ulsan, Korea). Raw (R), roasted (Rs), and spent (S) coffee grounds were obtained from BATKONG coffee stores (Seoul, South Korea). Potassium persulfate (MW = 2370.32, ≥99.13%) 2,2-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS, MW = 548.68, ≥98%), (hydrochloric acid, 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl, (DPPH, ≥ 99.13%), ethanol (≥ 95%; MW = 46.07), was procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The minced pork was bought from a local supermarket (Seoul, Korea) for packaging testing.

Three types of CDs were prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal strategy using ground (R), roasted (Rs), and spent (S) coffee grounds according to previously reported methods (Khan et al., 2023e; Min et al., 2022). For this, 1 g each of coffee grounds was kept in a Teflon-lined reactor with 50 mL of distilled water, sonicated for 5 min to mix thoroughly to obtain a good dispersion, and then heated for 7 h at 180 °C in a muffle furnace. (Fig. S1 Supporting Information). After coming back to 25 °C, the resultant solution mixtures were sieved via a Whatman filter (100 µm pore size) and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (12298g) for 10 min to obtain a clear supernatant without unreacted substances. Then, the supernatant was sieved through a nylon filter (Whatman membrane filter, diameter=25 mm, 0.22 µm pore size) to obtain CD solutions. The yields of R-, Rs-, and S-CDs were 4.4 mg/mL, 4.6, and 5.1 mg/mL, respectively. The yellowish-brown solutions of CD were stored at 4 °C before further characterization.

CNF-based active packaging films incorporated with three types of CDs (R-, Rs-, and S-CDs) were made via a solution casting strategy (Fig. S1) as per the reported approach (Riahi et al., 2023). First, CNF (4 g) was slowly mixed with distilled water (200 mL) to dissolve it, then glycerol (30 wt% based on CNF), a plasticizer, was introduced and vigorously stirred for 30 min at 90 °C. Afterward, 3 wt% (based on CNF) of CDs were poured dropwise to produce the CNF-solution mixture and stirred for 2 h at 45 °C, and then these 200 mL of obtained solutions were cast on a glass plate coated with Teflon sheet (24 cm × 30 cm) and kept at 22 ± 2 °C for drying. Then, the obtained films were conditioned for 48 h at 25 °C and 50% relative humidity (RH) before further analyses. In addition, the film of neat CNF was fabricated following the same protocol deprived of adding CDs. The fabricated films were assigned as neat CNF, CNF@R-CD, CNF@S-CD, and CNF@Rs-CD films based on the type of added CDs. The CNF@R-CD, CNF@S-CD, and CNF@Rs-CD films contain 120 mg of R-, Rs-, and S-CDs, respectively, as functional fillers.

Analytical methods for the properties of the produced CDs and fabricated films, including chemical composition, size and shape, functional groups, optical and color properties, surface morphology, WVP (water vapor permeability), mechanical properties, WCA (water contact angle), and other properties, are providing in the Supporting Information.

The radical scavenging or antioxidant efficacy of the synthesized CDs and CNF-based films were assessed using DPPH free radical scavenging assays and ABTS cation radical scavenging activity (Khan et al., 2023g). For the antioxidant activity of ABTS, an aqueous solution of 7 mM ABTS was treated with a solution of potassium persulfate (2.45 mM) and incubated overnight in the dark. After that, predetermined concentrations of the R-, Rs-, and S-CDs and 50 mg of dried film samples were treated with 10 mL of ABTS solution and left for 20 min at room temperature before measuring the absorbance at 734 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series spectrophotometer, Seoul, Korea). For the determination of DPPH free radical scavenging activity, 0.4 mg of DPPH was dissolved in 100 mL of methanol, and a similar amount of the CDs and film samples were added to a 10 mL methanolic solution of DPPH. After 20 min of incubation, the absorbance was recorded at 517 nm. The antioxidant activity was calculated as follows:

The antibacterial activities of the R-, S-, and Rs-CDs were determined against Escherichia coli O157: H7 ATCC 43895, Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 23235, Listeria monocytogenes ATCC 15313, and Salmonella enterica, (Korean Collection for Type Culture, KCTC, Seoul, Korea). The agar disk and well diffusion strategies were used to evaluate the bactericidal effects following the previously reported method (Khan et al., 2023d).

The antibacterial property of the films against Escherichia coli O157: H7 ATCC 43895 and Listeria monocytogenes ATCC 15313 was evaluated by assessing the number of viable colony count (TVC) method in a similar manner reported in our previous investigations (Riahi et al., 2023). In brief, broth cultures of L. monocytogenes and E. coli were inoculated aseptically in brain heart infusion (BHI) media and tryptic soy broth (TSB), respectively, then cultured for 16 h at 37 °C with gentle shaking. After that, the inoculum was diluted to obtain 105-106 CFU/mL and 10 μL of the obtained diluted inoculum was poured into the 20 mL BHI and TSB broths. Further, 100 mg of film sample sterilized for 20 min against UV radiation were added to the 20 mL of the diluted culture medium at 37 °C with gentle stirring at 100 rpm. The control was prepared without the film sample and used as reference one. Finally, the test samples were taken out from the flask after a predetermined interval (0, 3, 6, 9, and 12 h), plated on an agar plate after serial dilution, and counted to evaluate the total number of viable bacterial colonies (Log CFU/mL).

TBARS analysis was carried out to determine the degree of lipid oxidation according to the method of Borzi et al. (Borzi et al., 2019). For this, 5 g of minced pork was treated with the solution of trichloroacetic acid (10%) and thoroughly mixed for 3 min using a vortex mixture. The solution mixture was filtered to collect the filtrate. After that, 5 mL of the filtrate from the homogenized samples was combined with 5 mL of 20 mM thiobarbituric acid. The UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to test the solution's absorbance at 532 nm after it had cooled in an ice bath. 1,1,3,3-Tetramethoxypropane was used to create a standard curve, and TBARS results were given as mg MDA/kg sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of films in pork preservation
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared films (neat CNF, CNF@R-CD, CNF@S-CD, and CNF@Rs-CD films) were used to package minced pork to investigate the practicality of these films. For packaging purposes, a packet of two CNF-based films (8 cm × 8 cm) was made using a heat sealer. In the prepared CNF pouch, 10 g of raw minced pork was packed and sealed with polyethylene wrap and stored at 4 °C (low-temperature incubator) for 21 days. As a control test, minced pork stored in a Petri dish without wrapping was used. Throughout the storage period, pH, TBARS (thiobarbituric acid reactive substances), color, quality, and bacterial growth were evaluated for the control and experimental groups. Quantitative measurements of color and pH changes were performed for visual aesthetic evaluation, and TBARS evaluation was evaluated to demonstrate the actual quality of packaged meat. The growth of bacteria in packaged samples during the storage period was assessed by analyzing total viable colony count (TVC) values. For this purpose, 5 g of stored meat samples were homogenized in 45 mL of 0.1% peptone water using a stomacher (BagMixer® 400, Interscience, France) at 37 °C for 180 s at 100 rpm. Then, serially diluted homogenous aliquots (1 mL) were spread evenly onto 3 M™ Petrifilm counting plates. TVC data based on colony counts were expressed as log CFU/g over a 48-h incubation period at 37 °C (Hong et al., 2024). Test samples were quality assessed every 7 days during storage, and the experiment was repeated for quality analysis three times for each packaging group.

All experiments were performed in triplicates, and the experimental data were presented as the mean ± standard deviation. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, and the significance was determined (p < 0.05) with Duncan’s multiple range test using the SPSS statistical analysis system (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

The microstructure and size distribution of the developed CDs were observed, and the outcomes are shown in Fig. 1a. As revealed in TEM images, all three types of CDs depicted a round shape, and the particles observed to have sizes within the quantum range (Khan et al., 2023e). The particle size distributions of CDs (Fig. 1b) were measured by fitting the histogram to a Gaussian model. The sizes of R-, Rs-, and S-CDs were less than 10 nm with average diameters of 5.8 ± 1.0, 6.4 ± 0.9, and 6.2 ± 0.8 nm, respectively. Additionally, the surface zeta potential (Fig. 1c) showed that the R-, Rs-, and S-CDs have distinct negative surface charges of approximately − 7.9 mV, − 9.2 mV, and − 5.3 mV, respectively, expecting their high potential dispersibility in aqueous solution (Priyadarshi et al., 2021).

UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained to assess the optical characteristics of the CDs, and the outcomes are depicted in Fig. 1d-f. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs in the 200–600 nm range show similar absorption phenomena to previously investigated biomass-derived CDs (Riahi et al., 2024a). Owing to the quantum confinement consequence, aqueous solutions of all three CDs under UV irradiation at 365 nm emitted intense blue luminescence (inset, Fig. 1d-f). The rapid color change is caused by the emission of light in the UV spectrum, which is characteristic of CDs. The source of the CD and synthesis method have a significant impact on the degree of fluorescence intensity emitted upon exposure to ultraviolet light (Pandiyan et al., 2020). The SPR (surface plasmon resonance) peak of the CD solution exposed the n-π * transition of the C−OH or CO bond assured by a sharp absorption peak centered at 270 nm, which validates the fabrication of CDs via carbonization of different forms of coffee ground (Pandiyan et al., 2020). The high UV–vis absorption band of the synthesized CDs indicates significant UV shielding properties, indicating their potential for use in UV-blocking food packaging (Ezati et al., 2023a).

Additionally, to evaluate the luminescence performance of R-, Rs-, and S-CDs, their fluorescence (FL) emission responses at different excitation wavelengths (290–500 nm) were recorded (Fig. 1g-i). As shown in Fig. 1d-f, the R-, Rs-, and S-CDs exhibit maximum FL emission at 441 nm, 440 nm, and 443 nm, respectively, at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm (optimal value). Also, the emission response unveiled a typical excitation wavelength dependence behavior, with the emission wavelength being red-shifted at longer wavelengths of excitation (Fig. 1g-i) (Yang et al., 2014). The most common cause of such harmonic emission may be defects on the surface that produce tunable emissions in diverse energy states (Khan et al., 2023f). Also, it has been suggested that different fluorescence properties, such as the existence of diverse emission positions on the surface and changes in CD size, may contribute to the tunable emission of CDs (Baker et al., 2010). The actual fluorescence phenomenon that gives rise to excitation wavelength-dependent emission is complex to explain and is not yet established. Additionally, the measured photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) values for R-, Rs-, and S-CDs were 9.6%, 9.7%, and 10.4%, respectively, suggesting a new route for fluorescent CD fabrication from waste carbon raw materials. The PLQY values obtained for biomass-derived CDs appear to be sufficient to use the synthesized CDs as functional fillers for the development of food packaging films.

Fluorescence lifetime measurements (TRPL) were performed to determine the effect of changes in coffee composition on the fluorescence lifetimes of the three CDs. The TRPL spectra of the three CDs are exposed in Fig. S2, and Table S1 (Supporting Information) lists the faster and slower average decay constant values of the CQDs (Tammina et al., 2023b). The average decay constant of CDs was 4.8–5.5 ns, and the decay constant values of these CDs were not significantly different depending on the type. Optical and TRPL observations showed that all three types of coffee grounds were capable of producing CDs with approximately similar efficiencies.

The CDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis to evaluate the functional groups and constitutional elements and structural compositions, and the outcomes are exposed in Fig. 2a. In the FTIR spectra of R-, Rs-, and S-CDs, the -OH groups are owing to the existence of a broad peak at 3289 cm−1 (Fig. 2a). The IR peaks at 2918 and 2872 cm−1 corresponded to the methylene groups (-CH2). Further, the asymmetric and symmetric mode of vibrations of the C-O-C, O-H (phenolic hydroxyl group), and C-N (amide group) bonds can be ascribed by the occurrence of IR peaks at 1048, 1278, and 1369 cm−1, respectively (Khan et al., 2023c). The absorption around 1455, 1652, and 1762 cm−1 were attributed to -CH3, CC, and CO, respectively (Khan et al., 2024). The resulting FTIR findings showed that some of the absorption peaks of all three tested CDs may overlap or be reduced due to vibration mode and stretching of CC, C-H, N-H, and -OH groups in similar IR regions.

XPS analysis confirmed the presence of C1s, N1s and O1s peaks as major constitutional elements corresponding to binding energies of 283.08, 402.08 and 533.08 eV for the R-, Rs-, and S-CDs (Fig. 2b) (Khan et al., 2023c). The deconvoluted XPS investigation of C1s revealed the occurrence of CC and C-N bonds corresponding to 284.8 eV and 285.4 eV, while the C-O and CO confirmed through the components peaks at 286.5 and 287.4 eV, respectively (Fig. S3a) (Chu et al., 2023). The deconvoluted form of O 1 s depicted the occurrence of an O-CO bond at a binding energy of 531.1 eV, an O-CO bond at 532.0 eV, and 532.9 eV of binding energies a C-O bond (Fig. S3b). Additionally, Fig. S3c shows that the deconvoluted form of the N 1 s spectrum reveals the occurrence of pyridinic N at 398.7 eV, amino N at 399.5 eV of binding energy, and pyrrolic N at 400.6 eV.

Radical scavenging action is most frequently employed to evaluate the efficacy of materials in scavenging or neutralizing free radicals (Priyadarshi et al., 2021). Fig. 2c, d shows the DPPH and ABTS radical neutralizing efficacy of the R-, Rs-, and S-CDs (Khan et al., 2024). Different concentrations of CDs were mixed with methanolic DPPH and aqueous ABTS solutions, and the corresponding absorbance was measured at 517 and 734 nm after 20 min of incubation. The free radical neutralizing efficacy of the R-, Rs-, and S-CDs were 31.4 ± 0.3%, 35.3 ± 0.3%, and 40.5 ± 0.4% against both ABTS and 5.6 ± 0.2%, 8.6 ± 0.3%, and 12.7.5 ± 0.2% for free radicals of DPPH, respectively, at 12.5 μg/mL of concentration. As the concentration of R-, Rs-, and S-CD amplified from 12.5 to 100 μg/mL, the DPPH scavenging activity reached 62.7 ± 0.3%, 76.2 ± 0.4%, and 67.8 ± 0.6%, whereas the ABTS neutralizing activity decreased, respectively. The results were 99.7 ± 0.4%, 100.0 ± 0.4%, and 100.0 ± 0.5% (Fig. 2c, d). The reason may be the CDs’ higher solubility in the aqueous solution of ABTS than in the methanol solution of DPPH owing to the CDs’ hydrophilic nature. The results suggested almost 99 to 100% neutralization of ABTS•+ radicals and more than 62 to 76% DPPH• radicals, possibly using R-, Rs-, and S-CD as antioxidants. The antioxidant activity index (AAI) of the R-, Rs-, and S-CDs in the DPPH• assay were 4.4 ± 0.01, 5.7 ± 0.01, and 4.7 ± 0.03, respectively, while that of the ABTS•+ assay was 25.0 ± 0.2, 31.5 ± 0.1, and 25.7 ± 0.2. In general, an AAI of less than 0.5 is considered to have weak antioxidant activity, an AAI of 0.5 to 1.0 is considered to have moderate antioxidant activity, an AAI of 1.0 to 2.0 is considered to have strong antioxidant activity, and an AAI greater than 2.0 is considered to have very strong antioxidant activity (Scherer & Godoy, 2009). The antioxidant activity of R-, Rs-, and S-CDs corresponded to the abundance of oxygenated surface functional groups of the CDs. Regarding the radical scavenging mechanism of CDs, it has been widely discussed that DPPH• and ABTS•+ can be reduced and quenched through the transfer of H• from the CDs’ -OH moieties DPPH-H and ABTS-H (Murru et al., 2020).

The antibacterial activity of the R-, Rs-, and S-CDs was assessed toward E. coli, S. aureus, L. monocytogenes, and S. enterica by determining the inhibition zone using agar disk and well diffusion assays (Fig. 2e, f and Table 1). Additionally, both well diffusion assays and disk diffusion assays were performed on the same agar plate. A clear difference in results was observed with the agar well diffusion (Fig. 2f) assay showing a larger area of inhibition compared to the disk diffusion assay (Fig. 2e) for tested bacterial strains capable of demonstrating susceptibility/resistance (Table 1). The main reason is that readily available CDs can easily diffuse from aqueous dispersions onto agar media, whereas CDs bombarded from discs may take time to diffuse onto agar media after being released from cellulose disks. However, both methods tested confirmed that the synthesized CDs could efficiently provide antibacterial activity in both free (aqueous) and bound (disc) forms. The inhibition zone test outcomes showed that the bactericidal efficacy of the CDs was stronger toward the Gram-positive bacteria (L. monocytogenes and S. aureus) than the Gram-negative bacteria (S. enterica and E. coli), which corresponded to the structural differences in the bacterial cell wall (Khan et al., 2023e). The Gram-positive bacteria’s cell wall has a single thick peptidoglycan layer that promotes interaction with CDs, allowing CDs to enter the cell nucleus more straightforwardly. However, interactions with CDs are more difficult for Gram-negative bacteria due to the thin peptidoglycan layer with an outer cell wall (Khan et al., 2023e). Regarding the bactericidal mechanism of CD, early studies suggested that CD binds to the bacterial cell surface and transfers oxygen molecules, inducing reactive oxygen species (ROS) to generate OH• (free radicals of hydroxyl) and forming singlet molecular oxygen (1O2), impacting the oxidative or antioxidant balance which in turn abolish the biomolecules of bacteria and cause cell lysis (Khan et al., 2023a). Similar antibacterial activity results were observed for CDs made from other biomass, such as Kohlrabi peel and other food processing wastes (Khan et al., 2024; 2023a).

It is generally known that the abilities of CDs are impacted by the carbon source from which they are synthesized (Ezati et al., 2022a; Khan et al., 2023a). Based on the consequences of the analysis of the physicochemical, surface, and optical characteristics of the CDs, all three types of coffee (raw coffee, roasted coffee, and spent coffee) have formed blue luminescent properties and similar functional properties, which may be attributed to that the core carbon structure remains the same in all three forms of coffee. Other than temperature treatment, no specific compounds are required during the roasting and preparation of coffee beverages, suggesting that the core carbon structure remains intact without significant changes. In all three forms of coffee powders, the carbon core was identical, and the synthesized CDs showed almost similar types of functional properties. The occurrence of similar functional moieties (hydroxyl, carboxy, and amide groups) observed in FTIR analysis, TEM micrographs, and zeta potential confirmed the CD distribution in the ˂10 nm range with excellent stability in aqueous solutions. Optical property evaluation also confirmed the UV–vis light screening of these CDs in similar regions, and emission response tests confirmed nearly similar maximum FL intensities at the same optimal excitation wavelength (370 nm). Also, the three types of CDs showed similar functional properties of antioxidant and antibacterial activity. In conclusion, it was found that CDs manufactured from coffee powder had similar characteristics regardless of the type of coffee powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties and characterization of the film

ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra ( Fig. 3a) of the neat CNF, CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, and CNF@S-CD films were recorded to assess the surface functional groups of the film. For the neat CNF film, the broad peak around 3210–3415 cm−1 is indicated by the vibrational mode of stretching of O-H groups. However, for the CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, and CNF@S-CD films, this broad peak was separated into two peaks. That is, the absorption around 3179 cm−1 corresponds to the N-H bond, while the absorption near 3340 cm−1 represents the -OH bond of the H2O (Wagh et al., 2023). The peaks of all films located around 2897 cm−1 correspond to the vibrational C-H group’s stretching. The peaks around 1376–1363 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration modes of aromatic structure (CC) and amide-II/-NH (-CO groups) groups, respectively (Ezati et al., 2023a), whereas 1672 and 1604 cm−1 peaks position indicated the stretching vibration of carbonyl, and CC groups, respectively (Althomali et al., 2022). The peaks at 1025 and 1165 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of C-O-C and C-C pyranose of cellulose, respectively. Further, the β-glycosidic linkage of cellulose can be indicated by the peak at 850 cm−1 (Soni et al., 2015). For the CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, and CNF@S-CD films, the presence of IR peaks was almost similar owing to the overlapping -OH, -N-H, CO, C-C, and C-H vibrational modes stretching in the nearly similar IR region. However, slight shifting and changes in the characteristic peak intensities of the CD-incorporated films compared to the neat CNF film could be because of the physical interactions (H-bonds and van der Waals interactions) among the CNF polymer backbone and the phenol groups of CDs (Ezati et al., 2023a; Wagh et al., 2023). These FTIR results suggest that the inclusion of CDs in the CNF solution does not impact the chemical structure of the composite matrix but that the CDs are distributed throughout the polymer matrix and form well-mixed composites through physical interactions.

Fig. 3b shows the TGA (thermogravimetric analysis) thermograms of the neat CNF, CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, and CNF@S-CD films. TGA thermograms indicated that all the CNF composite films were moderately stable up to the temperature of 150 °C and also showed that the films depicted nearly analogous multistep thermal degradation patterns (Asmaly et al., 2015; Soni et al., 2015). The first weight decomposition was noticed at around 70–150 °C due to the evaporation of intermolecular H-bonded and absorbed water molecules from the film (Asmaly et al., 2015; Kim et al., 2021; Soni et al., 2015). The second major thermal degradation occurred at around 287 °C due to the polymer backbone and glycerol decomposition (Wagh et al., 2023). The final thermal decomposition of the films occurred starting at 400 °C due to complete depolymerization or dehydrogenation of the polymer backbone (Pereira et al., 2015).

The TGA test results also showed that a slight change was recorded in the thermal degradation of the composite films after the incorporation of R-, S-, and Rs-CDs into the CNF matrix. In the first step of the thermal decomposition, the films containing CDs lost faster in weight because the hydrophilic carbon dots captured higher moisture content. Similarly, compared to the neat CNF film, the CDs-added films exhibited slightly lower third thermal decomposition, which is likely to form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups of CNF, resulting in a slight increase in the thermal stability of the film. The increased thermal strength of the CD-incorporated films is believed to be due to synergistic interactions (hydrogen bonding) among the CDs’ functional groups and the CNF chain (as shown in SEM) (Khan et al., 2024).

XRD examination was executed to investigate films’ phase recognition and crystalline morphology (Khan et al., 2024), and the findings are shown in Fig. 3c. Two diffraction peaks corresponding to the typical diffraction patterns (110 and 002) of cellulose I appeared in the XRD patterns of the neat CNF, CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, CNF@S-CD films at 15.1° and 22.9° (Ezati et al., 2022b; Jiang et al., 2016), indicating the CNF’s semi-crystalline characteristics (Roy et al., 2020). This might be due to the concentration of CDs used to engineer the films being inadequate to produce crystallized structures in the composite films (Ezati et al., 2022b). Additionally, the CD-incorporated films showed similar diffraction patterns compared with the neat CNF film, demonstrating the uniform distribution of CDs within the composite matrix. The homogenous dispersal of the CDs in the polymer matrices can be associated with the amorphous and non-crystalline characteristics of the CDs (Ezati et al., 2022b).

Furthermore, the bond structure, elemental status, and chemical composition of the neat CNF, CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, and CNF@S-CD films were assessed through the XPS spectra. The survey and high-resolution XPS spectra were recorded, and the outcomes are presented in Fig. 3d-g. The C 1 s and O 1 s were the main constitutional elements for the neat CNF film, showing the peak position of 285.1 and 533.1 eV binding energies, respectively. However, for the CD-incorporated CNF films, the additional N 1 s peak positioned at ∼399.2 confirmed the existence of the CDs in the CNF matrices (Fig. 3d). The atomic concentration of nitrogen (N 1 s) in the CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, and CNF@S-CD films was 0.9%, 1.1%, and 1.0%, respectively (Table S2, Supporting Information). The deconvoluted C 1 s spectra of all films showed four component peaks. The peak at 283.8 eV of binding energy is indicated by the bonding groups of C-H/C-C with the sp2 orbital. The other split peaks at 285.2, 285.9, and 287.2 eV allocated to the bonding structure of CC/C-N, C-O, and carbonyl (C = O) or O-C-O, respectively (Fig. 3e) (Khan et al., 2023h; Riahi et al., 2023). Fig. 3f shows that the split O 1 s spectra of the CNF, CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, and CNF@S-CD films, verifying the formation of C-O-C/C-O-H, CO, and O-C-O bonds due to the three split peaks noticed around binding energies of ∼531.9 eV, 530.6 eV, and 532.4 eV, respectively (Zhang et al., 2015). For the CD-incorporated CNF films, N 1 s spectra can be split into three component peaks describing the presence of C-N-C, N-H, and-(C)3, which can be attributed by the peaks positioned around 398.3, 399.9 eV, and 399.1 binding energies, respectively (Fig. 3g) (Khan et al., 2023g).

The surface and cross-sectional view of the fabricated films were evaluated by recording FE-SEM images, as presented in Fig. 4a. For the neat CNF film, a complete, unruptured smooth surface with fine wavy clumps was observed, which may be due to the fibrous nature of the polymer matrix (Ezati et al., 2023a). However, incorporating CD as a functional filler did not change the structural morphology and caused the construction of a smoother surface similar to a neat CNF film. FE-SEM analysis results show that no lumps, cavities, or surface cracks were found in CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, and CNF@S-CD, demonstrating that the incorporation of CD did not damage the surface appearance of the film. Owing to the hydrophilic behavior of CDs and the profusion of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional moieties on the surface, excellent compatibility of CD and CNF mixtures can be realized. Therefore, the incorporation of CDs results in strong interactions with the polymer matrix through H-bonds among surface functional moieties, which are accountable for the homogeneous blending of CDs within the CNF matrix. Additionally, cross-sectional micrographs showed an intact appearance without cavities, pores, ruptures, or pores, confirming the excellent compatibility and distribution of CDs in CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, and CNF@S-CD films.

EDS (energy dispersive X-ray) study was executed to record the elemental composition on the surface of the neat CNF and CNF@Rs-CD film, and the outcomes are illustrated in Fig. S4a, b (Supporting Information). Elemental mapping depicted that oxygen and carbon, the main components of the CNF matrix, were uniformly distributed across the film surface. However, the unvarying dispersal of additional elements (nitrogen) in the CD-incorporated CNF films confirms that the CDs were evenly disseminated in the composite matrix. The EDS spectrum also confirmed the presence of oxygen and carbon, the main elements of CNF. Additional nitrogen elements for CDs were incorporated into the CNF film, confirming the presence of CDs in the composite matrix.

The actual appearance of the CNF-based films is exposed in Fig. 4b, and their color findings (ΔE, L, a, and b) are presented in Table 2. The neat CNF film was very transparent with a significant lightness (L=90.8). The impregnation of CDs lowered the transparency of the CNF film pointedly (p < 0.05) without changing a-values significantly (p > 0.05) while increasing the b-value meaningfully (p < 0.05) owing to the yellow appearance of the CDs. Accordingly, the ΔE (total color change) of the film amplified meaningly by the loading of CDs.

Transparency and UV-screening characteristics are important packaging film properties usually determined by measuring light transmittance. The transmittance of light for CNF-based films was estimated by analyzing the UV–vis spectra at 200–800 nm (Fig. 4c). The neat CNF film revealed high transmittance of light through the recorded wavelength, but the introducing CDs meaningfully lowered the light transmittance of CNF-based films. The UV-blocking properties and transparency of the film were estimated by measuring the transmittance reading at T280 (280 nm) and T660 (660 nm), respectively (Table 2). The T660 and T280 of the control film (neat CNF) were 86.4% and 48.4%, signifying the transparent nature of film to visible and UV light; however, with introduction CDs, transparency lowered significantly (p < 0.05). It is worthwhile to note that the transparency of the film (T660) was marginally declined (86.4 to 80.4%), while the UV-blocking ability (T280) of the film amplified significantly (48.4 to 0.2%) with the loading of the CDs. The UV blocking ability of CNF-based films according to the UV wavelength range is revealed in Fig. 4c, d; the CD-incorporated CNF films have higher UV blocking abilities in each UV wavelength range compared to the film of neat CNF. The improved UV-blocking ability of CD-loaded films is owing to the fact that CDs absorb or reflect UV rays and impede the transmission of UV rays. In particular, this may be because of the strong UV screening ability of aromatic polyphenol moieties contained by the CDs produced with coffee powder (Khan et al., 2023g).

Neat CNF (cellulose nanofibers), CNF@R-CD (cellulose nanofibers, raw coffee ground carbon dots), CNF@Rs-CD (cellulose nanofibers, roasted coffee ground carbon dots), CNF@S-CD (cellulose nanofibers, spent coffee ground carbon dots).

The mechanical strength of fabricated films was evaluated by gauging stress-strain curves, TS (tensile strength), EM (elastic modulus), and EB (elongation at break), and the outcomes are shown in Fig. 5a-d. The stress-strain curves verified that the film’s load-bearing capability showed typical viscoelastic features of the films (Fig. 5a). The neat CNF film was flexible and fairly strong, with the TS of 103.1 MPa, the EM of 6.8 GPa and the EB of 4.6% (Fig. 5b-d). The introduction of the CDs amplified the TS (strength) of the film slightly (6.9–20.2%) (Fig. 5b), which is possibly due to H-bond interactions among CDs’ functional groups and CNF chains (Khan et al., 2024; Khan et al., 2023d). Likewise, the film stiffness (EM) was also increased significantly with introducing CDs (Fig. 5c). However, the flexibility (EB) of the film was not meaningly changed with introducing CDs (Fig. 5d).

The surface hydrophilicity of the CNF-based films was estimated by gauging the WCA, and the results are shown in Fig. 5e. The WCA of the neat CNF was 45.1°, signifying the hydrophilic nature of the film (Ezati et al., 2023b). The WCA was slightly decreased with introducing CDs, nevertheless, the decrease was not statistically substantial (p > 0.05). The downfall in WCA of the CD-incorporated films was owing to the hydrophilicity of CDs due to the presence of carboxyl or hydroxy groups (hydrophilic functional groups)(Khan et al., 2024).

The WVP of the CNF-based films is exposed in Fig. 5f. For the neat CNF film, WVP was gauged to be 9.1 ± 0.2 × 10−10 g.m/m2. Pa.s, consistent with other polysaccharide-based biopolymer films (Kim et al., 2022; Riahi et al., 2024b). The film’s WVP declined marginally with the loading of CDs, but the decrease was not statistically substantial (p > 0.05). As can be seen from the existing findings, the impregnation of hydrophilic components may not have a substantial impact on the WVP of hydrophilic biopolymer films (Huang et al., 2019; Suriyatem et al., 2018).

Fig. 5g shows the radical neutralizing action of the CNF-based films, executed by the ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging function. The ABTS and DPPH radical neutralization efficacy of the neat CNF film was negligible (7.3% and 5.4%, respectively), whereas the CD-incorporated films showed significantly improved antioxidant action in both ABTS and DPPH method (98.2–98.6% and 64.2–78.8%, respectively). The enhanced antioxidant action of the CD-incorporated CNF films was attributed to the strong radical neutralizing ability of the CDs (Fig. 2c, d) covered with reactive functional moieties such as -CO and -OH sites that block free radical chain reactions and scavenge free radicals (Etxabide et al., 2021; Khan et al., 2023d).

The antibacterial potential of the CNF-based films was evaluated toward the foodborne bacterial strains, E. coli and L. monocytogenes, and the outcomes are presented in Fig. 5h, i. As anticipated, the neat CNF film did not exhibit any bactericidal ability, while CNF films containing CDs showed clear antibacterial activity. For the CNF@R-CD, CNF@Rs-CD, and CNF@S-CD films, the E. coli growth after incubation of 12 h was 4.9, 5.1, and 4.8 log CFU/mL, and that of L. monocytogenes was 5.3, 6.4, and 5.2 log CFU/mL, respectively, inferior to that of the control group. The CD-incorporated CNF films also exposed more effective bactericidal effects against Gram-positive strains than the Gram-negative ones, as confirmed in the antibacterial test results of the CDs (Fig. 2e, f). Overall, compared to neat films, CNF films containing CDs exhibited significant bacteriostatic properties and demonstrated their potential use as antibacterial active packaging films. It is expected that CDs can be released from the film matrix and act as antimicrobial agents against foodborne pathogens and contaminating microorganisms.

Pork is prone to oxidation of lipids, proteins, and pigments and bacterial contamination even at low temperatures during processing, storage, and supply, so it is needed to employ packaging materials with bioactive outcomes to protect processed meat during transportation and storage (Yang et al., 2023). Quality changes in minced pork wrapped with the CNF-based films were evaluated during 21 days of storage at 4 °C, and the outcomes are exposed in Fig. 6. The meat kept without packaging film spoiled within 7 days with visible discoloration and a putrid smell and pork packaged with the neat CNF film spoiled at 14 days of storage. Inversely, pork packaged in CD-incorporated films appeared to have a more intact visual appearance over the entire storage period.

Changes in the pH and lipid oxidation values of the tested samples during the storage are depicted in Fig. 6b. The initial pH of the fresh minced pork was 5.71 and increased owed to the production of nitrogen-comprising compounds due to protein decomposition during storage, and the amino acid trimethylamine and sugar complex increased alkalinity, increasing the pH value (Wagh et al., 2023). However, pork packaged with CD-incorporated films showed a much slower pH increase compared to the unpackaged group and the neat CNF film packaging group. It is generally known that pork with a pH exceeding 7.0 begins to deteriorate (Huang et al., 2017). Lattermost of storage, the pH of the unwrapped and neat CNF film-wrapped groups increased to 7.3 and 7.2, respectively, while the pH of pork packaged with the CD-incorporated films remained below 6.9. Therefore, it can be seen that the film containing CD is effective in maintaining the eminence of packaged pork throughout storage.

TBARS assays were employed to execute the degree of lipid peroxidation of the packaged pork by determining the lipid peroxidation end product, malonaldehyde (MDA) (Lin et al., 2023), and the findings are presented in Fig. 6c. The TBARS data of fresh minced pork was 0.23 mg MDA/kg and amplified to 1.4 and 1.1 mg MDA/kg, respectively, during 21 days. The rise in TBARS findings is mainly due to the unsaturated fatty acids’ oxidation during storage (Lin et al., 2023). Inversely, the TBARS value of pork packaged with the CD-incorporated films remained below 0.4 mg MDA/kg at the storage end, lower than the control group by 60–67%. The results indicate that the CD-incorporated CNF films can delay the oxidation of pork and preserve the quality of meat.

Discoloration in meat products is another sign of spoilage, indicating that the item is unsafe to eat (Khan et al., 2023f). Discoloration of the packaged pork during storage was evaluated by measuring the redness (a-value) (Fig. 6d). The a-value of the meat without packaging and packaged with the neat CNF film decreased more rapidly than the CD-incorporated film packaged groups. The reason why the a-value of meat decreases during storage is that the bright red myoglobin, the meat pigment of fresh minced pork, changes to brown metmyoglobin through oxymyoglobin through oxidation (Gurunathan et al., 2022). The slow rate of change in the a-value of pork packaged with CD-incorporated CNF films is believed to be due to the higher antioxidant abilities of these films (Fig. 6g).

Measurement of the total viable colony (TVC) count is one of the most vital criteria for the quality evaluation of packaged meat (Khan et al., 2023d). As revealed in Fig. 6e, the early TVC finding of the pork was about 2.5 log CFU/g and increased to higher than 8.5 log CFU/g of pork in the control groups after 21 days of storage. Inversely, the meat packaged with the CD-incorporated films increased to 6.1–6.7 log CFU/g, which is still within the permitted maximum ranges (≥7.0 log CFU/g) (Mohebi & Shahbazi, 2017). The lower rate of increase in the TVC of pork wrapped with the CNF@CD-films is due to the potent antibacterial potential of the CDs added in the films to prevent the growth of microorganisms (Fig. 2e, f and Fig. 5h, i). Overall, it can be seen that CNF films containing CDs can efficiently preserve pork stored at 4 °C.

Three different types of coffee grounds (raw, roasted, and spent coffee grounds) were used to prepare carbon dots, and all CDs showed similar morphological, structural, and functional properties, so the spent coffee ground was found to be a more economical and environmentally-safe source for the production of CDs. The CDs were employed for the construction of multifunctional CNF-based active films for food packaging applications. The addition of CDs amplified the functional abilities, such as antioxidant, antibacterial, and UV-blocking, of the CNF film. The CD-incorporated films were employed for the packaging of minced pork to prolong shelf life and improve packaged pork quality. From the results of this study, it is expected that CD can be produced from spent coffee (which is usually discarded) with desired functional properties and used to produce eco-friendly active packaging materials. Utilizing used coffee ingredients or by-products can help reduce the amount of waste disposed of in landfills or incinerators, which not only minimizes the environmental impact of waste disposal but also promotes efficient use of resources. Therefore, this study introduces a feasible method of recycling food waste and opens the way for its use as a new carbon-based material in the field of active packaging.
